---
name: socialpredict-go-architecture-governance
description: Enforce SocialPredict Go service-boundary governance for backend/API work. Use when implementing or reviewing changes in backend handlers, domain services, repositories, auth service, or route wiring to prevent layer violations, direct DB leakage, and cross-domain coupling.
---

# SocialPredict Go Architecture Governance

## Workflow

1. Confirm scope is limited to `socialpredict/backend/**` and API contract artifacts.
2. Read `references/boundary-governance.md` before editing boundaries or wiring.
3. Run `scripts/run_boundary_checks.sh [repo-dir]`.
4. Treat any new boundary violation as a blocker and stop implementation until fixed.
5. Report command output and violating file paths in the task note or review.

## Current PR Execution Scope

- Keep execution on PR #581 lineage and backend/API-only paths.
- Block new direct DB access in handlers/auth outside the approved exception list.
- Avoid frontend work and infrastructure rollout in this execution wave.

## Future-State Kubernetes Design Context (Guidance Only)

- Keep domains extractable into independently deployable services.
- Keep concrete wiring centralized so service extraction remains straightforward.
- Preserve interface-first boundaries so health/readiness and observability evolve cleanly later.

## Resources

- `references/boundary-governance.md`: rules, exceptions, escalation expectations.
- `scripts/run_boundary_checks.sh`: deterministic boundary and import checks.

---
name: socialpredict-go-code-quality-guardrails
description: Apply Go quality guardrails for SocialPredict backend/API work. Use when touching backend Go files to enforce formatting, vet hygiene, and backend boundary-safe coding practices before review or verifier handoff.
---

# SocialPredict Go Code Quality Guardrails

## Workflow

1. Confirm changed paths stay in backend/API scope.
2. Read `references/code-quality-checklist.md` for required quality gates.
3. Run `scripts/run_quality_guardrails.sh [repo-dir] [base-ref]`.
4. Resolve all blocking findings before handoff.
5. Include command output and touched-file list in the verification notes.

## Current PR Execution Scope

- Apply quality checks to backend/API files changed in the PR #581 wave.
- Enforce `gofmt` and `go vet` as non-optional gates.
- Avoid broad stylistic refactors unrelated to the current change set.

## Future-State Kubernetes Design Context (Guidance Only)

- Preserve configuration hygiene and observability-friendly error handling for eventual service extraction.
- Keep code changes diagnosable under distributed service operation.
- Maintain quality standards that support future independent service rollout.

## Resources

- `references/code-quality-checklist.md`: required gates and escalation criteria.
- `scripts/run_quality_guardrails.sh`: changed-file gofmt, go vet, and boundary hygiene checks.
- Companion tool skills for deeper or tool-specific passes:
  - `socialpredict-go-gofmt`
  - `socialpredict-go-vet`
  - `socialpredict-go-staticcheck`
  - `socialpredict-go-golangci-lint`
  - `socialpredict-go-cyclomatic-complexity`

---
name: socialpredict-go-testing-reliability
description: Run deterministic Go reliability checks for SocialPredict backend/API changes. Use when implementing or reviewing backend domain/service/repository/handler changes, diagnosing flaky test behavior, or preparing verification evidence.
---

# SocialPredict Go Testing Reliability

## Workflow

1. Keep execution limited to backend/API changes on PR #581 lineage.
2. Read `references/testing-reliability-guide.md` for deterministic test expectations.
3. Run `scripts/run_reliability_suite.sh [repo-dir] [pkg-pattern]`.
4. Repeat tests until results are deterministic or escalate as flaky-risk.
5. Log command outputs and failure triage notes in the task note.

## Current PR Execution Scope

- Prioritize service-level and integration-relevant backend tests for touched packages.
- Keep verification focused on changed backend/API behavior.
- Do not introduce unrelated test framework refactors.

## Future-State Kubernetes Design Context (Guidance Only)

- Preserve tests that remain valid when domains are extracted into separate services.
- Favor contract and integration checks that verify service boundaries, not in-process assumptions.
- Keep failure diagnostics actionable for later distributed tracing and observability workflows.

## Resources

- `references/testing-reliability-guide.md`: deterministic test policy and triage checklist.
- `scripts/run_reliability_suite.sh`: repeatable test, OpenAPI validation, and vet checks.

# SocialPredict Backend Boundary Governance

## Enforceable Rules (Current PR #581 Scope)

1. Handlers and `internal/service/auth` must call domain services/facades, not concrete repositories.
2. Domain packages (`backend/internal/domain/**`) must not import `backend/internal/repository/**` directly.
3. Repository packages must not import handlers.
4. Composition-root wiring belongs in `backend/server/server.go` and container/bootstrap code.

## Approved Legacy Exceptions (Do Not Expand)

- `backend/handlers/admin/adduser.go`
- `backend/handlers/stats/statshandler.go`
- `backend/internal/service/auth/loggin.go`
- `backend/handlers/cms/homepage/repo.go`
- `backend/server/server.go`

Policy: no new files may be added to this exception list.

## Escalate Immediately

- New cross-domain coupling that bypasses service interfaces.
- Any handler/auth direct DB use outside the exception list.
- Required change outside backend/API scope.

## Future-State Design Context (No Infra Changes Now)

Maintain boundaries so domains can become independently deployable services with their own readiness, configuration, and observability behavior in a later Kubernetes rollout phase.

# SocialPredict Go Quality Checklist

## Blocking Gates (Current PR #581 Scope)

1. `gofmt -l` must return no files for changed backend Go files.
2. `go vet ./...` must pass in `backend/`.
3. No new direct DB usage in handlers/auth outside the approved legacy exception list.
4. Verification output must be captured with exact commands and results.

## Escalation Triggers

- `go vet` failure indicating correctness or concurrency risk.
- Newly introduced direct DB usage outside approved files.
- Required remediation that exceeds backend/API scope.

## Future-State Design Context (No Deployment Execution Here)

Bias changes toward clean configuration boundaries and diagnosable behavior so services can be split and operated in Kubernetes later without rewrites.

# SocialPredict Testing Reliability Guide

## Deterministic Verification Policy

1. Run backend tests with `-count=1` to avoid cached pass artifacts.
2. Re-run the same suite at least twice for changed packages.
3. Treat non-reproducible failures as flaky-risk and document environment assumptions.
4. Include OpenAPI validation whenever API behavior may be affected.

## Reliability Triage Checklist

- Confirm the failing test maps to changed backend/API behavior.
- Separate deterministic code defects from environment-dependent failures.
- Note exact package, command, and failing assertion.
- Escalate unresolved flakes before marking a wave complete.

## Future-State Design Context (No Infra Rollout in This Task)

Prefer tests that remain meaningful after domain extraction to independently deployable Kubernetes services.

---
title: Background Jobs
document_type: production-notes
domain: backend
author: Patrick Delaney
updated_at: 2026-05-01T11:50:49Z
updated_at_display: "Friday, May 01, 2026 at 11:50 AM UTC"
update_reason: "Refresh the deferred jobs draft against landed readiness and startup-writer seams on upstream main at 051aac6."
status: draft
---

# Background Jobs

## Starter Draft Status

This is a lower-priority starter draft.

It exists to capture the likely future direction for asynchronous processing without confusing that work with the higher-priority runtime, DB, monitoring, and validation concerns described in the active production notes.

The current posture is explicit:

- background jobs are not part of the current production-hardening slice
- correctness comes before async offloading
- operational visibility comes before retry-heavy worker systems
- money-moving flows should not be pushed into background jobs to compensate for weak transaction boundaries

This draft was refreshed on Friday, May 01, 2026 against upstream `main` at `051aac6b2fefa5634b8c98cc38caf52acf0043a9`. The backend now has liveness and readiness probes, explicit startup-writer gating, and better request-boundary failure handling, but it still has no worker topology, no idempotency model, and no retry or outbox ownership.

## Executive Direction

If SocialPredict later introduces background-job infrastructure, it should do so as a narrow support system for explicitly idempotent, non-request-critical work.

That means:

1. Keep the active backend request path synchronous for accounting-sensitive flows such as betting, selling, and market resolution.
2. Introduce background processing only for tasks that are safe to decouple from the request path.
3. Treat retries, dead-letter handling, job ownership, and observability as prerequisites, not afterthoughts.
4. Avoid adding Redis, worker pools, cron frameworks, or queue tables just because they sound operationally mature.
5. Keep any future async system small and explicit rather than turning it into a second application architecture.

## Why This Is Deferred

The live backend still has more urgent concerns:

- the serving runtime baseline is stronger than the older draft assumed, but deployment still runs one HTTP-serving shape with no worker role
- atomic accounting-sensitive workflow boundaries still need more explicit hardening outside the place-bet path
- the runtime still lacks worker-specific signals such as lag, replay, retry, or dead-letter visibility
- no queue, outbox, scheduler, or worker ownership model exists yet in the codebase

Adding queues or workers before those concerns are stronger would add a new failure domain before the current system is fully ready to support it.

## Current Code Snapshot

### Runtime prerequisites are stronger, but still not worker-ready

The backend now has:

- `STARTUP_WRITER` mode in [runtime/startup_mutation.go](/workspace/socialpredict/backend/internal/app/runtime/startup_mutation.go)
- startup mutation and verification behavior in [startup_mutation.go](/workspace/socialpredict/backend/startup_mutation.go)
- liveness and readiness probes in [server.go](/workspace/socialpredict/backend/server/server.go)

That is useful baseline infrastructure, but it is still serving-process infrastructure. It does not define a queue contract, worker lifecycle, retry semantics, or async ownership model.

### There is no live background-job subsystem in the backend

The active backend does not currently have:

- a `jobs/` or `workers/` package
- a Redis queue runtime
- a cron or scheduler subsystem
- a job table or outbox pattern in the application code
- a second worker deployment topology in the repo

The current backend is still one primary server process.

### Current important flows are synchronous on purpose

Important flows currently execute inline through request and domain paths, including:

- `POST /v0/bet`
- `POST /v0/sell`
- market resolution flows

That is important because it means correctness and failure semantics are still concentrated in the request path rather than hidden behind async infrastructure.

The place-bet path now has stronger transaction behavior, but that still does not justify moving money-sensitive workflows behind background retries or workers.

### Deploy and workflow topology assumes one backend runtime shape

The current Docker and workflow topology publishes and deploys the backend as one primary HTTP-serving binary:

- [docker/backend/Dockerfile](/workspace/socialpredict/docker/backend/Dockerfile)
- [docker-compose-prod.yaml](/workspace/socialpredict/scripts/docker-compose-prod.yaml)
- [docker.yml](/workspace/socialpredict/.github/workflows/docker.yml)

There is no parallel worker deployment contract yet, which is another reason to keep this note deferred.

## Candidate Future Uses

If background jobs are introduced later, plausible early candidates are:

- email delivery
- periodic or triggered cache refreshes
- scheduled derived snapshots that are safe to recompute
- export generation
- non-critical notification fan-out

## Candidate Early Non-Targets

The following should not be early background-job targets:

- account-balance correctness
- bet placement correctness
- sell settlement correctness
- market-resolution correctness
- any flow where partial success would break system economics

## Preconditions Before Background Jobs

Before the backend should prioritize background-job infrastructure, it should first have:

- an operationally enforced startup and shutdown contract for more than one runtime role
- stronger readiness and monitoring posture for worker-specific failures
- explicit idempotency rules for candidate async tasks
- a clear retry and dead-letter policy
- visibility into failure, replay, and lag behavior
- at least one candidate task that is valuable, decouplable, and clearly non-financial

## Relationship To Other Notes

This note is intentionally downstream of:

- [04-database-layer.md](/workspace/socialpredict/README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/04-database-layer.md)
- [08-deployment-infrastructure.md](/workspace/socialpredict/README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/08-deployment-infrastructure.md)
- [09-monitoring-alerting.md](/workspace/socialpredict/README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/09-monitoring-alerting.md)
- [12-database-caching.md](/workspace/socialpredict/README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/12-database-caching.md)

Longer-term queue and worker ideas now belong in [FUTURE/07-long-term-background-jobs.md](/workspace/socialpredict/README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/FUTURE/07-long-term-background-jobs.md).

            "agent": "codex",
            "design_posture": "fowler",
            "notes": "Used the Evans, Fowler, and Martin designer-agent reviews to reconcile current-state claims against /workspace/socialpredict main at 9b22484 and update stale design-plan assertions."
        }
    ],
    "summary": {
        "one_sentence": "Refresh the SocialPredict backend design plan against the landed W01-W07 backend hardening baseline, with remaining work focused on legacy cleanup, clearer domain boundaries, transaction coverage outside place-bet, and production verification policy.",
        "business_outcomes": [
            "Reduce architectural fragility in a prediction market backend that is moving from hobby-grade operation toward professional reliability.",
            "Improve extraction readiness so high-availability and fault-tolerant deployment patterns remain achievable later without rewriting the active slice.",
            "Make backend behavior more predictable for operators and clients by clarifying ownership boundaries, auth behavior, and API contracts.",
            "Preserve 100% financial and accounting correctness by treating economics policy as an immutable, accounting-sensitive input rather than a live mutable control surface.",
            "Improve deploy, restart, and incident diagnosis by unifying runtime logging ownership, OpenTelemetry-aligned correlation, and health/readiness boundaries.",
            "Make multi-replica startup safer by clarifying migration and seed ownership, readiness semantics, and database-runtime lifecycle responsibilities before adding caching or other optimization layers.",
            "Make request-boundary security behavior more explicit across environments by clarifying rate limiting, auth failure ownership, JWT runtime expectations, and CORS or proxy assumptions.",
            "Preserve fast boundary-local verification in the live backend and add source-of-truth DB/runtime checks only where SQLite cannot credibly model behavior."
        ],
        "proposed_direction": "Treat the landed configuration, runtime observability, failure recovery, database-runtime, security, API-contract, and verification work as current code reality on socialpredict:main. Continue from that baseline by tightening remaining clean-architecture seams: auth transport leakage, misplaced persistence adapters, legacy route error shapes, setup/util compatibility bridges, sell/resolve transaction policy, and opt-in Postgres verification coverage."
    },
    "problem": {
        "statement": "The backend has now landed the cascading W01-W07 modernization baseline on socialpredict:main. Runtime DB configuration owns env normalization, pool sizing, connection lifetimes, SSL posture validation, readiness ping, and close behavior; startup mutation mode separates writer migrations/seeds from non-writer migration verification; startup mutation failure is fatal; request-boundary panic recovery, request correlation, shared 405/429/500 runtime failure writers, rate-limit JSON failures, trusted-proxy configuration, runtime security config, OpenAPI alignment, and targeted verification work are present. The design problem has shifted from adding those seams to accurately governing what remains: setup and util compatibility paths, transport-shaped auth service APIs, the CMS homepage repository under a handler tree, persistence/model leakage in internal/domain/users/uniqueness.go, the GORM analytics repository under internal/domain/analytics, mixed legacy handler error responses, process-global DB compatibility access, sell-position and resolve-market transaction/concurrency policy, and Postgres-backed checks that exist but remain opt-in and incomplete.",
        "why_now": "The design plan had drifted behind the code after the seven-PR backend hardening cascade. If the plan continues to describe landed mechanisms as missing, future task planning will reopen solved problems and miss the real residual risks: transport leakage, misplaced adapters, legacy error contracts, operational enforcement of startup-writer policy, and source-of-truth verification gaps.",
        "out_of_scope": [
            "Full microservice decomposition or container rollout in this pass.",
            "Broad frontend or infrastructure redesign.",
            "Public route reorganization.",
            "Limited-scope login token redesign beyond current enforcement and response-shape cleanup.",
            "Bets-to-Trades rename.",
            "Caching or Redis rollout as part of the active database-layer slice.",
            "Long-term security-platform work such as MFA, RBAC, distributed rate limiting, request signing, or compliance-program implementation.",
            "A centralized `testing/` subsystem, shared `TestSuite` base architecture, or testcontainers-first rollout in the active slice."
        ]
    },
    "design_drivers": {
        "business_constraints": [
            "Preserve existing behavior while modernizing architecture in small steps.",
            "Keep current external setup endpoints and current public route organization stable during prerequisite waves.",
            "Avoid a flag-day rewrite because the platform is already live and operational assumptions must remain intact.",
            "Maintain 100% financial and accounting correctness while configuration and accounting-sensitive flows are modernized.",
            "Keep Postgres as the system of record for authoritative accounting state while caching remains deferred."
        ],
        "architecture_drivers": [
            "Extraction readiness for future service/container boundaries.",
            "Dependency direction from handlers to policy to repositories rather than hidden globals.",
            "Runtime/bootstrap configuration and application-policy configuration are different concerns with different owners.",
            "Validate runtime/bootstrap config once at startup and inject immutable snapshots for the process lifetime.",
            "Runtime/bootstrap should own DB pool, SSL, connection-lifecycle, readiness, and migration-invocation concerns rather than leaving them scattered across startup helpers and handlers.",
            "Runtime/bootstrap should also own deployment-sensitive security posture such as JWT signing-key presence, CORS defaults, trusted-proxy assumptions, and header/HSTS ownership rather than leaving them split across server and auth helpers.",
            "Runtime diagnostic logging, health/readiness signals, metrics, and tracing are separate operational concerns with separate owners and sequencing.",
            "Keep `backend/logger` as the sole runtime logging adapter with stable vocabulary and safe redaction, and consolidate request-boundary observability ownership before expanding richer observability surfaces.",
            "Use OpenTelemetry as the canonical telemetry model, correlation vocabulary, and preferred export direction while keeping `backend/logger` as the application-facing adapter.",
            "Own pre-handler failures and unhandled panics at the server or middleware boundary instead of relying on scattered `http.Error` calls or default library behavior.",
            "Security hardening should extend the existing `backend/security`, `server`, and auth-facing seams rather than starting a second security platform tree.",
            "Application-policy configuration should live behind an owned internal boundary instead of a singleton package.",
            "Repositories should remain edge translators between persistence models and internal domain types rather than regressing into generic `models.*` CRUD facades.",
            "Shared startup mutations such as migrations and one-time seeds need single-writer startup semantics rather than per-replica default execution.",
            "Accounting-sensitive flows need explicit atomic transaction and concurrency direction; compensation and metrics are not a substitute for correctness.",
            "Explicit, testable contract shaping at the HTTP boundary.",
            "Separate processed business failures from transport/runtime failures in the public contract.",
            "Keep public HTTP failure reasons separate from internal telemetry fields such as `error.type` and span status.",
            "Validation and sanitization are reusable request-boundary support concerns and should not leak into domain policy or be recopied ad hoc into handlers.",
            "Avoid generic automatic retry layers around accounting-sensitive write paths unless idempotency and transaction semantics are explicit.",
            "Long-term identity/security-platform ideas such as MFA, distributed rate limiting, and request signing remain deferred until the active runtime, failure, and auth seams are stable.",
            "Caching and Redis-related work are later optimizations and should not outrun correctness and runtime ownership."
        ],
        "quality_attributes": [
            "Availability",
            "Changeability",
            "Testability",
            "Fault isolation",
            "Recoverability",
            "Operational predictability",
            "Financial correctness",
            "Security consistency",
            "Contract clarity"
        ]
    },
    "current_state": {
        "shape": "As of socialpredict:main at merge commit 9b22484, the backend is a transitional monolith with several hardening seams already implemented. internal/app/runtime owns runtime DB/security/readiness/startup-mutator concerns; the server wires request-boundary security, runtime failure handling, docs, OpenAPI, and route families; domain code is mostly under internal domain packages, with repositories and adapters translating legacy models at the edges. Remaining current-state issues are narrower than the prior plan claimed: auth still exposes HTTP request-shaped service APIs and a JWT env fallback, some persistence adapters live in misleading packages, legacy route families still emit ad hoc errors, place-bet has a transaction and Postgres lock but sell/resolve flows still need explicit transaction policy, and real-Postgres verification exists but is env-gated rather than a default CI source of truth.",
        "pain_points": [
            "setup and util compatibility paths still need owner-by-owner retirement so they do not become new hidden boundaries.",
            "internal/service/auth still accepts *http.Request in service-facing APIs and keeps JWT signing-key fallback state, even though HTTP failure translation has moved toward typed auth outcomes plus handlers/authhttp.",
            "CMS homepage persistence lives under handlers/cms/homepage, internal/domain/analytics contains a GORM repository, and internal/domain/users/uniqueness.go imports GORM/models directly; these are now the concrete persistence-placement violations to track.",
            "Request-boundary runtime failure handling exists, but legacy handlers still mix ReasonResponse, ad hoc JSON, and http.Error paths.",
            "Startup writer mode exists, but production still needs an operational guarantee that exactly one writer performs shared startup mutations.",
            "Place-bet now has a transaction/unit-of-work seam and Postgres row-level locking; sell-position and market-resolution still need explicit transaction and concurrency policy.",
            "Postgres-backed verification has started for startup and place-bet transaction behavior, but remains opt-in/env-gated and incomplete for other DB-truthful behaviors."
        ],
        "constraints": [
            "The existing container and internal service pattern is real and should be extended rather than replaced.",
            "`backend/setup/setup.yaml` is the current source asset and should remain the source of truth during this slice.",
            "W01-W07 backend hardening work is already landed on socialpredict:main and should be treated as design baseline, not reopened as greenfield debate.",
            "The current backend still uses Gorilla Mux, GORM, and embedded assets; this slice should isolate those details better but not replace them.",
            "Historical docs under `backend/README/BACKEND/API` are informative but not fully current.",
            "Long-term security-platform ideas are intentionally deferred until the runtime, failure, and auth boundary seams are clearer.",
            "Caching and Redis-related work are intentionally lower priority than runtime DB ownership and accounting-sensitive correctness.",
            "The active slice should not introduce a centralized `testing/` subsystem, shared `TestSuite` base architecture, or testcontainers-first rollout."
        ]
    },
    "target_state": {
        "overview": "The active target remains an evolutionary monolith with explicit runtime seams and clearer business-domain language. Runtime/bootstrap owns DB configuration, pool and SSL posture, startup-writer mode, readiness, runtime diagnostic logging, request correlation, panic recovery, failure writers, and deployment-sensitive security settings. Application-policy configuration owns economics/frontend policy through immutable typed views. Request-boundary security owns validation, sanitization, rate limiting, forwarded-header trust, and security headers. Business domain slices own market, bet/position ledger, participant account, and analytics/reporting policy without treating HTTP or persistence models as native policy language. Repositories and adapter seams translate legacy models and hold transaction-scoped persistence. API/auth contract work maps typed outcomes to stable public reasons and OpenAPI. Verification remains package-local by default, with targeted Postgres checks for behavior SQLite cannot prove.",
        "boundaries": [
            {
                "name": "Runtime Bootstrap and Infrastructure",
                "kind": "module",
                "purpose": "Own process startup concerns such as env loading, runtime/bootstrap configuration validation, DB initialization, DB lifecycle, migration invocation, readiness checks, controlled seed bootstrapping, and deployment-sensitive security posture.",

item.text: The strongest fit is the CMS homepage rendering path: it is derived, recomputable, and non-financial, and it already runs inline during admin updates. I’m going to make that rendering operation an explicit service method while leaving the handler path synchronous.

The service seam is in place and the handler path still uses the same `UpdateHome` call, so runtime behavior remains inline. I’m formatting and running focused tests now.

019ded93-e03c-7cb1-a845-8635f0cadc03.message:
Completed WAVE13-JOBS-001.

Specialists used: `architecture-agent` for required architecture sign-off, and `verifier-agent` for final diff/validation sanity. Candidate selected: CMS homepage render/sanitize, because it is non-financial, derived from CMS input, and safely recomputable.

Changed:
- `handlers/cms/homepage/service.go`: added `RenderInput`, `RenderedContent`, and synchronous `Service.RenderContent`, then routed `UpdateHome` through that seam.
- `handlers/cms/homepage/service_test.go`: added coverage for markdown, HTML, and unsupported format render paths.

Required constraints held:
- No jobs package, queue, scheduler, worker, cron, outbox, Redis, or async runtime added.
- Runtime remains synchronous.
- New seam has no HTTP coupling.
- No route, OpenAPI, DB schema, or migration change.

Checks passed:
- Architecture boundary checks.
- `gofmt` wrapper on touched files.
- Reliability suite for `./handlers/cms/homepage` with workspace Go caches, including repeated tests, OpenAPI validation, and package vet.
- Verifier diff check and focused package test.

item.text:
task_uid: `019de369-df09-7c64-9c85-726295a4323c`

status: `completed`

Changed `handlers/cms/homepage/service.go` to expose CMS homepage render/sanitize as `Service.RenderContent(RenderInput)`, an HTTP-free, derived, recomputable service seam. `UpdateHome` still calls it inline, so runtime behavior remains synchronous and no jobs, queues, schedulers, workers, Redis, or async infrastructure were added.
